title: File Operations
content:
### touch — Create Empty Files or Update Timestamps

```bash
touch newfile.txt
touch file1 file2 file3   # create multiple
```

### mkdir — Create Directories

```bash
mkdir mydir
mkdir -p parent/child/grandchild   # create nested directories
```

### cp — Copy Files and Directories

| Option | Purpose |
|--------|---------|
| `-r` | Recursive (copy directories) |
| `-i` | Interactive (prompt before overwrite) |
| `-v` | Verbose (show what's being copied) |
| `-u` | Update (only copy newer files) |

```bash
cp file.txt backup.txt
cp -r /src /dest
```

### mv — Move or Rename

```bash
mv oldname newname          # rename
mv file.txt /tmp/           # move to directory
mv -i source dest           # prompt before overwrite
```

### rm — Remove Files and Directories

| Option | Purpose |
|--------|---------|
| `-r` | Recursive (delete directories) |
| `-f` | Force (no confirmation) |
| `-i` | Interactive (prompt each file) |

```bash
rm file.txt
rm -rf tempdir/        # DANGEROUS — deletes without confirmation
```

### ln — Create Links

```bash
ln file.txt hardlink       # hard link (same inode)
ln -s target symlink       # symbolic link (different inode)
```

### file — Determine File Type

```bash
file document.pdf
file /bin/ls
```
codeExample:
mkdir -p projects/website
cp -v index.html projects/website/
mv projects/website projects/site
rm -i temp.txt
file /bin/bash

quiz.question: What does `mkdir -p a/b/c` do?
options: Creates nested directories a, b, and c
Creates a single directory named a/b/c

quiz.question: What is the difference between `cp` and `mv`?
options: cp copies; mv moves or renames
cp deletes the original; mv keeps it
